DOME‑HDR

Dual‑Output Multi‑Exposure HDR Reconstruction via SDR Fusion and Gain Map Inverse Tone Mapping

Jinho Kim Jinwoo Kim Seon Joo Kim
Yonsei University, Republic of Korea
arXiv Code
black pointdynamic rangewhite point
UE
ME
OE

Three exposures span the scene from deep shadow to clipped highlight. DOME‑HDR fuses them into a display-ready SDR and expands it into a consistent HDR — one pipeline, two outputs.

Abstract

One pipeline, two consistent outputs

We propose DOME‑HDR, a dual-output multi-exposure HDR reconstruction framework that jointly produces a perceptually balanced SDR image and a consistent HDR image via gain map inverse tone mapping. Given three bracketed LDR inputs, DOME‑HDR first synthesizes a base SDR using a LoRA-adapted latent diffusion model. A dual cross-attention fusion module injects complementary structural and color cues from the under- and over-exposed images while anchoring on the mid-exposure for stability. The synthesized SDR then guides HPGM, our HDR Prior-guided Gain Map network, to predict a spatially varying gain map for reliable dynamic-range expansion. Evaluated on Kalantari, Tel, and Challenge123 with both full-reference and no-reference metrics, DOME‑HDR achieves state-of-the-art HDR reconstruction quality; ablations further confirm the effectiveness of dual cross-attention and SDR-guided gain map estimation.

Fusion

Dual cross-attention

Two independent attention branches inject under- and over-exposed cues onto the mid-exposure anchor, instead of averaging exposures before attention — so complementary detail is kept, not blurred away.

Gain map

HDR-prior guided (HPGM)

A U-Net predicts a residual gain map over a physically derived HDR prior built from all three exposures, plus a global scale — stabilizing dynamic-range expansion where a single SDR would clip.

Output

SDR & HDR, jointly

Both stages are optimized together to emit a display-ready SDR and its corresponding HDR — no tone-mapping operator to choose, and no error propagating from a frozen SDR stage.

Method

A good base SDR is the key to good HDR

A gain map can only scale an existing SDR signal — if the input SDR is saturated, no gain map can bring the detail back. DOME‑HDR jointly trains an SDR synthesizer and a gain-map estimator so the two never work against each other.

STAGE 1

Base SDR synthesis

Built on the UltraFusion diffusion backbone. The mid-exposure image is the structural anchor; dual cross-attention transfers UE and OE cues at multiple scales. Only LoRA adapters (rank 8, on the query and value projections) are trained — the U-Net and VAE stay frozen. A RAFT-based suppression module masks unreliable, motion-affected regions before fusion to curb ghosting.

STAGE 2

Gain map estimation

HPGM is a U-Net over an 11-channel input (base SDR, cross-exposure ratios, saturation masks). At the bottleneck it fuses a convolutional HDR-prior feature; edge-guided upsampling sharpens boundaries. Two heads then predict a residual gain map over the HDR prior together with a global HDR scale.

Results

Qualitative Results

Every comparison runs the same three exposure brackets through each baseline. Watch the highlight regions — signboards, sky, windows — where gain-map-only and cascaded methods lose detail first.

Quantitative Results

Numbers behind the pictures

HDR reconstruction on the combined test splits of Challenge123, Tel and Kalantari. Best per column highlighted; DOME‑HDR leads on fidelity and perceptual similarity while staying strong on no-reference quality.

No-reference metrics (CLIPIQA / NIQE / BRISQUE) computed on µ-law tone-mapped HDR images for fair comparison. ↑ higher is better · ↓ lower is better.
Limitation. Quality is bounded by how reliable the exposure cues are. Severe motion, large saturated regions, or complex illumination can degrade the fused SDR and, downstream, the gain estimate — so recovering extreme radiance variations in those regions remains difficult. The authors point to more expressive exposure representations as future work.
